note: State-level policy overlays rendered onto OpenLayers/Census Reporter vector tiles in Web Mercator.

overlays.title: Abortion policy environment (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: State abortion-policy environment categories from Guttmacher's interactive policy map.

overlays.title: Total abortion ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states are enforcing a total abortion ban with very limited exceptions. Anyone denied abortion access in their state must either overcome the logistical and financial hurdles of traveling out of state,  obtain medication abortion through a shield law provider,  navigate a self-managed  abortion or carry an unwanted pregnancy to term. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: 6-week ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states are enforcing a six-week abortion ban. Anyone denied abortion access in their state must either overcome the logistical and financial hurdles of traveling out of state,  obtain medication abortion through a shield law provider,  navigate a self-managed  abortion  or carry an unwanted pregnancy to term. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: 12-week ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states are enforcing a 12-week abortion ban. Anyone denied abortion access in their state must either overcome the logistical and financial hurdles of traveling out of state,  obtain medication abortion through a shield law provider,  navigate a self-managed  abortion  or carry an unwanted pregnancy to term. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: 15-week ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states are enforcing a 15-week abortion ban. Anyone denied abortion access in their state must either overcome the logistical and financial hurdles of traveling out of state,  obtain medication abortion through a shield law provider,  navigate a self-managed  abortion  or carry an unwanted pregnancy to term. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: 18–23-week ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states are enforcing an abortion ban between 18 and 23 weeks. Anyone denied abortion access in their state must either overcome the logistical and financial hurdles of traveling out of state,  obtain medication abortion through a shield law provider,  navigate a self-managed  abortion  or carry an unwanted pregnancy to term. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: 24–26-week ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states are enforcing an abortion ban between 24 and 26 weeks. Anyone denied abortion access in their state must either overcome the logistical and financial hurdles of traveling out of state,  obtain medication abortion through a shield law provider,  navigate a self-managed  abortion  or carry an unwanted pregnancy to term. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: No gestational ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states do not restrict abortion based on gestational duration. This is a critical protection that allows providers to offer abortion care throughout pregnancy. Learn more about state bans on abortion throughout pregnancy.

overlays.title: State constitution explicitly excludes abortion rights (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states explicitly exclude protections for abortion rights in the state constitution. This allows state policymakers to enact laws that infringe on residents’ bodily autonomy without state constitutional protection as a means for those laws to be challenged or blocked in state court.

overlays.title: Waiting period after counseling (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states force patients to undergo mandatory counseling that often includes incomplete or biased information and wait 24–72 hours after counseling to obtain an abortion. Scheduling this counseling creates additional barriers for patients and providers. Learn more about counseling and waiting period restrictions on abortion.

overlays.title: Waiting period after required in-person counseling (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states force patients to undergo mandatory in-person counseling that often includes incomplete or biased information and wait at least 24 hours after counseling to obtain an abortion. Mandating two clinic visits adds barriers for clinics and patients that may be insurmountable. The in-person requirement can affect use of telehealth for a medication abortion. Learn more about counseling and waiting period restrictions on abortion.

overlays.title: Forced ultrasound (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states require patients to have an ultrasound before obtaining an abortion. Ultrasounds in the first trimester are not usually medically necessary; requiring their use serves to dissuade patients from seeking an abortion and may add significant cost. Learn more about ultrasound requirements.

overlays.title: State Medicaid coverage ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] only allow state Medicaid funds to pay for abortion care under very narrow exceptions. This restriction forces Medicaid enrollees to overcome the financial barriers of paying for an abortion and other costs to obtain the procedure.

overlays.title: Private insurance coverage ban (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states restrict coverage of abortion in all private insurance plans, forcing people with private health insurance to overcome the financial barriers of paying for an abortion and other costs to obtain the procedure.

overlays.title: Medication abortion in-person requirement (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states require medication abortion to be provided in person, because of either an in-person dispensing requirement or a ban on telehealth provision. Medically unnecessary in-person requirements create numerous financial and logistical barriers for providers and patients. Learn more about restrictions on medication abortion.

overlays.title: Mailing abortion pills banned (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states ban providers from mailing abortion pills to patients. This restriction is not based on medical standards of care; the purpose is to assign criminal or civil liability to providers who mail abortion pills. Learn more about restrictions on medication abortion.

overlays.title: Medically unnecessary clinic regulations (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states have laws or policies that regulate abortion providers and clinics with the sole aim of making care more cumbersome or costly to provide. Learn more about unnecessary regulations of abortion clinics.

overlays.title: Parental consent required for a minor's abortion (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states force a minor seeking an abortion to obtain parental consent, often at least 24–48 hours before they can obtain care. These laws potentially put minors at risk and create additional barriers that may delay, dissuade or prevent a young person from seeking abortion care. Learn more about parental involvement in minors’ abortions.

overlays.title: Parental notice required for a minor's abortion (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states require a parent to be notified before a minor’s abortion, often requiring the notice to occur at least 24 hours prior to the procedure. Parental involvement laws potentially put minors at risk and create additional barriers that may delay or dissuade a young person from seeking abortion care. Learn more about parental involvement in minors’ abortions.

overlays.title: Only physicians can provide abortions (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states allow solely physicians to provide abortions, excluding other qualified health care professionals such as physician assistants, certified nurse-midwives and nurse practitioners. This restriction limits the pool of health care professionals who can provide care, reducing access and straining provider capacity.

overlays.title: Abortion reporting required (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states require health care providers to submit  reports to the state about all   abortions   provided , often requiring information that serves no clear public health purpose .  Some states  require  detailed reports, while others  only require aggregate reports or reports in only some circumstances.   Learn more about mandatory abortion reporting.

overlays.title: State constitution protects abortion rights (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states include protections for abortion rights in the state constitution. These protections provide a legal firewall against potential efforts by future state policymakers to restrict access to care and can be used to strike down previous restrictions.

overlays.title: State statute protects abortion rights (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states include protections for abortion rights in state statute. These laws   provide  additional  protection against  potential efforts by future state policymakers to restrict access to care.

overlays.title: State Medicaid funds cover abortion (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states have a policy or court order that directs state Medicaid funds to pay for abortions under all circumstances or in more circumstances than what is re quired by the Hyde Amendment . This is critical as the federal Hyde Amendment bars the use of federal Medicaid funds to pay for abortions except under very narrow exceptions.

overlays.title: Private health plans required to cover abortion (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states require abortion coverage in private health insurance plans. Guaranteeing insurance coverage of abortion is essential to ensure care is accessible and affordable.

overlays.title: Health care professionals besides physicians provide abortions (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states allow a range of qualified health care professionals to provide abortion care, including physician assistants, certified nurse-midwives and/or nurse practitioners. This measure increases the pool of abortion providers and enhances access.

overlays.title: State fund for abortion care (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states use state funds to help individuals pay for abortion services. Allocating specific funding for abortion care and related expenses is critical to expanding access to all who need it.

overlays.title: Protections to enter a clinic (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] have policies protecting abortion clinic staff, patients and anyone else entering a clinic from harassment and physical harm. These protections can include prohibiting specific acts of violence against abortion providers and patients or establishing a delineated physical space around clinics.

overlays.title: Shield law protecting providers (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] have   an abortion shield law in place, either through statute or executive order. Includes states with any type of shield law protection, which can vary in the scope and types of protections provide d . Learn more about shield law protections.

overlays.title: Shield law protecting providers extends to telehealth (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] s tates have an abortion shield law in place to protect abortion providers using telehealt h, regardless of the patient’s location. Learn more about shield law protections.

overlays.title: State law allows health care providers to list the facility name, instead of individual provider names, on medication abortion drug label (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states have a l aw in place allowing health care providers to list the facility name, instead of individual provider names, on medication abortion drug labels.  T hese law s  provide  additional  protection to health care providers, specifically shield law providers, who provide abort ion care.

overlays.title: Patient reproductive health data privacy (Guttmacher)
overlays.description: [Insert Number] states protect data privacy for patients seeking reproductive health care. This can include additional protections for patients’ health records, digital communications or other information that could be used by law enforcement, and blocking the use of geofencing, which refers to tracking people’s locations, within a certain perimeter around health care facilities.

overlays.title: Abortion law status (Center for Reproductive Rights)
overlays.description: After Roe Fell state abortion-law status categories.

01.label: Illegal
01.note: Alabama is enforcing its total abortion ban.

02.label: Protected
02.note: Abortion will remain legal in Alaska. In 1997, the state’s highest court recognized a fundamental right to “reproductive choice” under the Alaska Constitution.

04.label: Protected
04.note: The Arizona pre-Roe ban has been repealed. As a result of an amendment to the Arizona Constitution that protects abortion rights, the fifteen-week ban is enjoined. Abortions after fetal viability remain prohibited, with limited exceptions.

05.label: Illegal
05.note: Arkansas is enforcing its trigger ban to prohibit abortion entirely.

06.label: Expanded Access
06.note: Abortion will remain legal in California. The state's highest court recognized abortion rights under the California Constitution in 1969, four years before Roe. State law protects the right to personal reproductive decisions. In November 2022, Californians approved Prop 1, which explicitly adds abortion and contraception rights to the state constitution.

08.label: Protected
08.note: Abortion remains legal in Colorado. In 2022, Colorado enacted a statute recognizing abortion as a fundamental right, and in 2024, Colorado enshrined that right into the state’s constitution.

09.label: Expanded Access
09.note: Abortion will remain legal in Connecticut. State law protects abortion and Connecticut has enacted additional laws to expand abortion access.

10.label: Protected
10.note: Abortion will remain legal in Delaware. State law includes express protections for abortion and, in 2021, Delaware repealed its final pre-Roe ban.

12.label: Hostile
12.note: Florida is enforcing a six-week ban. On November 5, 2024, despite the support of majority of Florida voters, Amendment 4, which would have amended the state constitution to prohibit government interference with the right to abortion prior to viability, failed to meet the 60% threshold required to amend the Florida Constitution.

13.label: Hostile
13.note: Georgia is enforcing a 6-week abortion ban.

15.label: Expanded Access
15.note: Abortion remains legal in Hawaii. Hawaii has legalized abortion, and the state has enacted additional laws to expand access to abortion.

16.label: Illegal
16.note: Idaho is enforcing its trigger ban, which criminalizes all abortion with limited exception.

17.label: Expanded Access
17.note: Abortion remains legal in Illinois. In 2019, the Illinois legislature enacted a law establishing abortion as a fundamental right. The Illinois Supreme Court has also recognized the right to abortion under the state constitution.

18.label: Illegal
18.note: Indiana is enforcing a total abortion ban with very narrow exceptions.

19.label: Hostile
19.note: Abortion in Iowa is heavily restricted. As of July 2024, the state is enforcing a 6-week ban.

20.label: Protected
20.note: Abortion will remain legal in Kansas as long as the state constitution is not amended. In 2019, the state’s highest court ruled that a pregnant person’s right to personal autonomy is protected in the Kansas constitution, and, in 2022, voters overwhelmingly opposed a constitutional amendment to remove that protection.

21.label: Illegal
21.note: Kentucky is enforcing its trigger ban, which prohibits abortion at all stages of pregnancy.

22.label: Illegal
22.note: Louisiana is enforcing its trigger ban which prohibits abortion entirely and includes civil and criminal penalties.

23.label: Protected
23.note: Abortion will remain legal in Maine. State law protects the right to personal reproductive decisions. In April 2024, Maine further strengthened protections for reproductive and gender-affirming health care.

24.label: Expanded Access
24.note: Abortion rights in Maryland remain strongly protected, with constitutional guarantees for reproductive freedom.

25.label: Protected
25.note: Abortion remains legal in Massachusetts. The Massachusetts Supreme Court has recognized the right to abortion under the state’s constitution, and in 2021 Massachusetts passed comprehensive abortion rights legislation. In 2025, Massachusetts further strengthened protections for reproductive and gender-affirming care.

26.label: Protected
26.note: Abortion rights in Michigan remain strongly protected, with constitutional and statutory protections for reproductive freedom, including abortion care.

27.label: Expanded Access
27.note: Abortion will remain legal in Minnesota. The state’s highest court has recognized the right to abortion under the Minnesota Constitution and, in 2023, the state created a statutory right to reproductive freedom.

28.label: Illegal
28.note: Mississippi is enforcing its trigger ban, which prohibits abortion in nearly all situations.

29.label: Protected
29.note: Voters approved a 2024 amendment to the Missouri Constitution to protect reproductive freedom. On July 3, 2025, a state circuit court temporarily enjoined the state's trigger ban, gestational bans, and reasons bans, as well as other restrictions on abortion. However, requirements that only physicians provide abortion care and in-person, as well as targeted restrictions on medication abortion, have not been enjoined and are currently in effect.

30.label: Protected
30.note: Abortion remains legal in Montana. In November 2024, voters passed a constitutional initiative that expressly codified protections to abortion rights into the Montana Constitution.

31.label: Hostile
31.note: Nebraska is enforcing a 12-week abortion ban, with limited exceptions. On November 5, 2024, Nebraskans voted to enshrine the 12-week ban in the state constitution and rejected an amendment that would have protected abortion until viability.

32.label: Protected
32.note: Abortion remains legal in Nevada. In 1990, Nevada voters passed a referendum safeguarding the state’s law legalizing abortion, and, in 2024 voters approved an amendment to the Nevada Constitution to protect abortion rights, which requires a second vote on November 3, 206, to be effective.

33.label: Not Protected
33.note: Abortion remains accessible in New Hampshire but without legal protection.

34.label: Expanded Access
34.note: Abortion remains legal in New Jersey and is recognized as a fundamental right under both state statute and the state constitution.

35.label: Not Protected
35.note: Abortion will remain accessible in New Mexico. In 2023, the state enacted laws to ensure access to reproductive healthcare, including abortion, and gender affirming care.

36.label: Expanded Access
36.note: Abortion remains legal in New York. In 2019, New York enacted comprehensive abortion rights legislation, expanding access to abortion care in the state, and beginning in 2022 enacted additional protections for abortion providers, patients, and helpers. In 2024, voters approved an amendment to the New York Constitution that prohibits discrimination based on pregnancy outcomes and reproductive healthcare and autonomy.

37.label: Hostile
37.note: North Carolina is enforcing a twelve-week abortion ban, including additional burdensome restrictions, which took effect July 1, 2023. Although the current governor is supportive of abortion rights, the legislature is hostile to abortion.

38.label: Illegal
38.note: North Dakota is enforcing a total abortion ban with limited exceptions.

39.label: Protected
39.note: Ohioans have amended the state constitution to protect reproductive decision making.

40.label: Illegal
40.note: Oklahoma is enforcing a pre-Roe ban that prohibits abortion entirely, except when "necessary to preserve" the life of a pregnant person.

41.label: Expanded Access
41.note: Abortion remains legal in Oregon. State law protects abortion and Oregon has enacted additional laws to expand abortion access.

42.label: Hostile
42.note: Abortion remains accessible in Pennsylvania. The Pennsylvania Commonwealth Court, an intermediate appellate court, has held that the Pennsylvania Constitution guarantees a fundamental right to reproductive autonomy—the highest court in the state has not weighed in on this ruling. The current governor is supportive of abortion rights, but numerous medically unnecessary restrictions make it difficult to access abortion care in the state.

44.label: Protected
44.note: Abortion will remain legal in Rhode Island. In 2019, Rhode Island enacted a law to protect abortion and repealed various existing abortion restrictions.

45.label: Hostile
45.note: South Carolina is enforcing a six-week abortion ban after the state Supreme Court declared the ban constitutional on August 23, 2023.

46.label: Illegal
46.note: South Dakota is enforcing its trigger ban, which criminalizes abortion. In November 2024, voters failed to approve an amendment to the state constitution that would have protected abortion rights in the first trimester.

47.label: Illegal
47.note: Tennessee is enforcing its trigger ban, which prohibits abortions at all stages of pregnancy, with limited medical emergency exceptions, and criminalizes violations of these prohibitions.

48.label: Illegal
48.note: Texas is enforcing its trigger ban, which prohibits abortion and includes civil and criminal penalties for providers.

49.label: Hostile
49.note: Utah is enforcing its 18 week ban while its trigger ban is enjoined.

50.label: Expanded Access
50.note: Abortion will remain legal in Vermont. In 2019, Vermont enacted a statutory provision protecting abortion as a fundamental right, and in 2022, Vermonters approved a state constitutional amendment that enshrines the right to reproductive freedom in the state constitution.

51.label: Not Protected
51.note: Abortion will remain accessible in Virginia, but without legal protection. In 2020, Virginia repealed numerous medically unnecessary restrictions on abortion access.

53.label: Expanded Access
53.note: Abortion remains legal in Washington. State law protects personal reproductive decisions, and Washington has enacted policies to expand abortion access.

11.label: Protected
11.note: Abortion is protected in the District of Columbia. However, due to the District's unique legal status, the U.S. Congress could attempt to prohibit or severely restrict abortion in the absence of Roe.

54.label: Illegal
54.note: West Virginia has enacted a total ban on abortion

55.label: Hostile
55.note: Wisconsin is enforcing a twenty week LMP ban.

56.label: Protected
56.note: Now that the Supreme Court has overturned Roe, the Wyoming Supreme Court held the total ban unconstitutional. However, on March 9, 2026 the Governor signed a six-week ban into law, which is currently enjoined.
